explicit_text: I arrived at Ellis Island on April 19, 1910, coming from Russia with my family. We traveled on the ship Furnessia, coming from Glasgow after leaving Odessa. Ellis Island was our gateway to America, where we went through Customs before starting our new life.

explicit_text: I was born in Elizabethgrad, Russia, near the Inhul River in 1908. Life there was difficult, especially for Jewish families like mine, because of the Pogroms and restrictions in the Pale of Settlement. My family decided to leave Russia to seek freedom and safety in America.

explicit_text: After arriving in America, my family first stayed at 57 East 98th Street in Manhattan. We lived there while adjusting to life in a new country and learning English, since we only spoke Yiddish. Later, we moved back to Manhattan after spending some time in New Haven, Connecticut, and I worked hard there as a bookkeeper.

The Pogroms and anti-Jewish attacks there made life dangerous, so my parents decided to leave for America. The Pale of Settlement was the place we escaped from in search of freedom.

explicit_text: I was determined to get an education despite my family's financial struggles. After dropping out to help support my family, I convinced my parents to let me return to school. I graduated from Julia Richmond High School, which was a proud moment for me.

explicit_text: My family eventually moved to the Bronx, where I worked as a bookkeeper because I was good with numbers. It was also where I met my future husband at a Halloween party when I was 21. We raised our family there and built our life together.

explicit_text: The Pogroms were violent anti-Jewish attacks in Russia that made life unbearable for my family. These attacks in the Pale of Settlement were a major reason my parents decided to leave Russia. Escaping the Pogroms meant we could finally live without fear in America.

explicit_text: The United States gave my family and me a chance to live freely and pursue our dreams. Though the transition was difficult, with language barriers and financial struggles, we persevered. America allowed me to work, get an education, and raise a family in a way that would have been impossible in Russia.
